perturbation.original_text: Article 12 – Disposal Within ninety (90) days of termination of this Agreement, the ICC Court may request the disposal of the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information disclosed to the Member. Disposal means execution of reasonable measures to return or destroy all materials and copies including electronic data. Destruction shall be confirmed in writing. Disposa l shall be effected within thirty (30) days of the request being made.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 12 – Disposal Within ninety (90) days of termination of this Agreement, the ICC Court may request the disposal of the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information disclosed to the Member. Disposal means execution of reasonable measures to return or destroy all materials and copies including electronic data. Destruction shall be confirmed in writing. Disposa l shall be effected within ten (10) days of the request being made.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows 30 days for disposal after a request. Shortening this to 10 days may be unreasonably short, especially for large volumes of data. Although this NDA is governed by French law, principles of reasonable time for compliance exist in most jurisdictions, especially with GDPR and data privacy considerations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 12(3) GDPR states that the controller shall provide information on action taken on a request under Articles 15 to 22 to the data subject without undue delay and in any event within one month of receipt of the request. While this isn't directly about disposal, it sets a standard of 'one month' for responding to data requests. Reducing the disposal timeframe to 10 days may be considered an undue restriction, potentially conflicting with the spirit of GDPR's emphasis on providing reasonable time for data handling.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 12 GDPR – Transparent information, communication and modalities for the exercise of the rights of the data subject - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability 1 The controller shall take appropriate measures to provide any information referred to in Articles 13 and 14 and any communication under Articles 15 to 22 and 34 relating to processing to the data subject in a concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form, using clear and plain language, in particular for any information addressed specifically to a child. 2 The information shall be provided in writing, or by other means, including, where appropriate, by electronic means. 3 When requested by the data subject, the information may be provided orally, provided that the identity of the data subject is proven by other means. 1 The controller shall facilitate the exercise of data subject rights under Articles 15 to 22 . 2 In the cases referred to in Article 11 (2), the controller shall not refuse to act on the request of the data subject for exercising his or her rights under Articles 15 to 22 , unless the controller demonstrates that it is not in a position to identify the data subject. 1 The controller shall provide information on action taken on a request under Articles 15 to 22 to the data subject without undue delay and in any event within one month of receipt of the request. 2 That period may be extended by two further months where necessary, taking into account the complexity and number of the requests. 3 The controller shall inform the data subject of any such extension within one month of receipt of the request, together with the reasons for the delay. 4 Where the data subject makes the request by electronic form means, the information shall be provided by electronic means where possible, unless otherwise requested by the data subject. If the controller does not take action on the request of the data subject, the cont
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the time for data disposal from 30 days to 10 days, potentially conflicting with the spirit of GDPR's emphasis on providing reasonable time for data handling. The Law Explanation points to Article 12(3) GDPR, which sets a standard of 'one month' for responding to data requests. Although the scraped law snippet does not directly address data disposal, it does state, "The controller shall provide information on action taken on a request under Articles 15 to 22 to the data subject without undue delay and in any event within one month of receipt of the request." This indicates that a month is considered a reasonable timeframe, and reducing the disposal time to 10 days could be viewed as creating an inconsistency with this standard, especially considering data privacy considerations.

perturbation.original_text: Article 14 – Dispute Resolution This Agreement is governed by, and shall be construed in accordance with the laws of France. Any dispute arising from or in connection with this Agreement shall be resolved by both Parties or their representatives, if any, through consultation and negotiations at the request of either Party by written notice to the other Party. Failing a resolution within three weeks following the notice mentioned in the above paragraph, the Tribunal de Grande Instance de Paris shall have exclusive jurisdiction to hear and decide any suit, action or proceedings, and to settle any disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 14 – Dispute Resolution This Agreement is governed by, and shall be construed in accordance with the laws of France. Any dispute arising from or in connection with this Agreement shall be resolved by both Parties or their representatives, if any, through consultation and negotiations at the request of either Party by written notice to the other Party. Failing a resolution within one week following the notice mentioned in the above paragraph, the Tribunal de Grande Instance de Paris shall have exclusive jurisdiction to hear and decide any suit, action or proceedings, and to settle any disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text gives three weeks for resolution through consultation before resorting to litigation. Reducing this to one week is an unreasonably short timeframe, potentially conflicting with general principles of good faith negotiation and dispute resolution, especially regarding cross-border disputes.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1104 of the French Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1104 of the French Civil Code states that contracts must be negotiated, formed and performed in good faith. Reducing the negotiation period to one week could be construed as a breach of good faith, especially in complex disputes where a reasonable period for negotiation is expected under French law. A one-week timeframe might be considered insufficient to conduct meaningful negotiations, potentially violating this requirement.

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the dispute resolution timeframe from three weeks to one week. The Law Explanation states that this could be a breach of good faith under Article 1104 of the French Civil Code, because one week may be insufficient for meaningful negotiation. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a stub, it generally indicates that French Contract Law exists within the Code Civil. The Perturbation explanation also states that the reduced timeframe could conflict with general principles of good faith negotiation, particularly for complex or cross-border disputes. The Inconsistencies definition, which states that a legal contradiction arises when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, applies here.

perturbation.original_text: Article 10 – Survival of Obligations Upon termination of this Agreement, the Member shall stop making us e of Confidential Information or Proprietary Information. The Parties’ obligations under this Agreement shall survive indefinitely or to the longest extent permitted by the applicable laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 10 – Survival of Obligations Upon termination of this Agreement, the Member shall stop making us e of Confidential Information or Proprietary Information. The Parties’ obligations under this Agreement shall survive for six months.
perturbation.explanation: While the original clause allows for indefinite survival of confidentiality obligations (or the longest period permitted by law), limiting it to only six months is problematic. For sensitive information, a six-month window is an unreasonably short time, going against trade secret protections.
perturbation.law_citation: Article L151-1 of the French Commercial Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Article L151-1 of the French Commercial Code defines a trade secret as information that is not generally known or readily accessible, has commercial value because it is secret, and is subject to reasonable steps by the person lawfully in control of the information to keep it secret. Limiting the survival of confidentiality obligations to six months may be seen as failing to take 'reasonable steps' to protect the trade secret, particularly if the information retains its commercial value beyond that period, thus conflicting with the spirit of this law aimed at trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California's Dreaming Of Tougher Health-Care Privacy - Healthcare - United States ARTICLE 6 August 2018 California's Dreaming Of Tougher Health-Care Privacy DP Day Pitney LLP More Contributor Day Pitney LLP is a full-service law firm with more than 300 attorneys in Boston, Connecticut, Florida, New Jersey, New York and Washington, DC. The firm offers clients strong corporate and litigation practices, with experience on behalf of large national and international corporations as well as emerging and middle-market companies. With one of the largest individual clients practices on the East Coast, the firm also has extensive experience assisting individuals and their families, fiduciaries and tax-exempt entities plan for the future. Explore Firm Details The law applies to manufacturers of consumer health wearables and apps, for example, and its reach extends to companies located anywhere in the U.S. that do business in California. United States Food, Drugs, Healthcare, Life Sciences Day Pitney LLP Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. An August 2 article in Bloomberg Law's Health Law & Life Sciences News , "California's Dreaming of Tougher Health-Care Privacy," discussed the California Consumer Privacy Act, a new law that gives consumers the right to know how much of their personal data is being collected by companies and the right to have that data deleted upon request, among other provisions. Day Pitney's Eric Fader was quoted in the article. The law applies to manufacturers of consumer health wearables and apps, for example, and its reach extends to companies located anywhere in the U.S. that do business in California. Eric told Bloomberg Law that other states are likely to enact their own privacy laws modeled after California's, and that differences in wording among the various states' laws may make compliance with all of them a complex process for a large company. For more articles and regular updates on legislative changes, regulatory developments and other news of interest to businesses, professionals and investors in the healthcare industry, please subscribe to Day Pitney's mailing lists. perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for indefinite survival of confidentiality obligations, potentially aligning with trade secret protection, whereas the changed contract limits this survival to six months. The Law Explanation states that limiting the survival of confidentiality obligations to six months may be seen as failing to take 'reasonable steps' to protect a trade secret, particularly if the information retains its commercial value beyond that period. This is because the original indefinite term, or the longest period permitted by law, is replaced with a fixed and potentially insufficient six-month term. While the provided snippets do not contain specific trade secret legal information, the Law Explanation specifically raises an issue with the alteration of the term and trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: Article 9 – Term and Termination This Agreement enters into force by signing of all Parties and can be terminated by either Party with immediate effect by giving a written notice to the other Party. This Agreement ends automatically upon expiry of the Member’s term of office.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 9 – Term and Termination This Agreement enters into force by signing of all Parties and can be terminated by either Party without notice. This Agreement ends automatically upon expiry of the Member’s term of office.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires written notice for termination. Removing this and allowing termination 'without notice' might contradict principles of fairness and good faith negotiation within contract law in France.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1104 of the French Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: French Civil Code Article 1104 mandates that contracts must be negotiated, formed, and performed in good faith. Terminating a contract without any prior notice may be viewed as a violation of this good faith requirement. This is because it deprives the other party of the opportunity to prepare for the termination or take steps to mitigate any potential harm resulting from the termination, which goes against the principle of fair dealing inherent in French contract law.
perturbation.justification: The original text required written notice for contract termination, while the changed text allows termination 'without notice.' The Law Explanation states this could violate French Civil Code Article 1104, which mandates contracts be performed in good faith. Terminating without notice deprives the other party of the opportunity to prepare or mitigate harm, contradicting fair dealing inherent in French contract law. The perturbation explanation supports the Law explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Article 2 – Obligation to keep Confidential and Restrictive Use The Member shall: a) not disclose any Confidential Information or Proprie tary Information unless expressly authorised by the ICC Court; b) use any Confidential Information or Proprietary Information exclusively for the purpose of its collaboration with the ICC Court and not for its own or anyone else’s benefit; c) keep secu re, confidential and hold all Confidential Information and Proprietary Information with no less a degree of care as is used for the Member’s own confidential or Proprietary Information and at least with reasonable care; and d) not amend, vary, develop, im prove, enhance (“Derivation”) the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information except as otherwise approved in writing by the ICC Court. In addition, ICC Court members are only permitted to disclose Confidential information or Proprietary informati on for the sole purpose of conflict checks. They must only disclose such information to the person duly authorized to run conflict checks within their respective law firm or company. ICC Court members undertake that their firm or company will not use the C onfidential information or Proprietary information for any other purpose and remain responsible for ensuring that such information is immediately destroyed upon conclusion of the conflict check.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 2 – Obligation to keep Confidential and Restrictive Use The Member shall: a) not disclose any Confidential Information or Proprie tary Information unless expressly authorised by the ICC Court; b) use any Confidential Information or Proprietary Information exclusively for the purpose of its collaboration with the ICC Court and not for its own or anyone else’s benefit; c) keep secu re, confidential and hold all Confidential Information and Proprietary Information with reasonable care; and d) not amend, vary, develop, im prove, enhance (“Derivation”) the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information except as otherwise approved in writing by the ICC Court. In addition, ICC Court members are only permitted to disclose Confidential information or Proprietary informati on for the sole purpose of conflict checks. They must only disclose such information to the person duly authorized to run conflict checks within their respective law firm or company. ICC Court members undertake that their firm or company will not use the C onfidential information or Proprietary information for any other purpose and remain responsible for ensuring that such information is immediately destroyed upon conclusion of the conflict check.
perturbation.explanation: The original requires a degree of care no less than the member's own confidential info. Removing this implies a lower standard, potentially conflicting with trade secret and confidentiality protection expectations.
perturbation.law_citation: Article L151-1 of the French Commercial Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Article L151-1 of the French Commercial Code defines a trade secret and requires 'reasonable steps' to keep it secret. Removing the requirement for the member to use 'no less a degree of care as is used for the Member’s own confidential or Proprietary Information' weakens the obligation to protect the information. It may indicate a failure to take 'reasonable steps' to protect trade secrets, and this conflicts with French trade secret law.

perturbation.justification: The change in the contract weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The original text requires 'no less a degree of care as is used for the Member’s own confidential or Proprietary Information', while the changed text only requires 'reasonable care.' The Law Explanation states that Article L151-1 of the French Commercial Code requires 'reasonable steps' to keep trade secrets. By removing the requirement to use the same degree of care as for the member's own information, the modified text potentially fails to meet the 'reasonable steps' standard, creating a legal contradiction as it weakens the protection of trade secrets.
